name: Mina
short_description: BLM rockhounding near Mina in Nevada's high desert. Find chalcedony, quartz, and jasper specimens on public land with no permit required. Open year-round for personal use collection.
description:
Mina sits in Mineral County at nearly 6000 feet elevation in Nevada's Basin and Range country, offering accessible rockhounding on BLM land. This region is known for quality chalcedony and quartz specimens that formed during extensive Tertiary volcanic and hydrothermal activity across central Nevada. The gentle rolling terrain around Mina makes it suitable for collectors of all skill levels, with numerous washes and hillsides exposing colorful agate, jasper, and chalcedony pieces.

The high elevation keeps this area cooler than lower Nevada desert sites, making spring and fall particularly pleasant for collecting. Unlike many fee dig operations, this BLM land allows free personal use collection under the 25 lb per day limit. The landscape is classic Great Basin desert with sparse sagebrush, open vistas, and good natural light for spotting gemstones.

Collectors typically focus on washes and bajadas where water action has concentrated lighter specimens. The chalcedony here often displays translucency and subtle banding, while jasper and agate specimens range from small nodules to substantial pieces. With no commercial activity allowed, this remains a quiet, low-impact collecting area preferred by serious rockhounders seeking quality material without crowds.
history: The Mina area sits within the Basin and Range physiographic province where Tertiary-age volcanic activity (roughly 20-30 million years ago) deposited extensive silica-rich hydrothermal fluids. These created the secondary quartz and chalcedony veins and replacements found throughout central Nevada today. Historic silver mining activity in the Tonopah district to the east demonstrates the mineral-rich geology of this entire region.
tips: Search washes and bajada margins where seasonal water flow concentrates lighter gemstones. Look for translucent white to pale tan chalcedony and banded agate pieces ranging from thumbnail-sized to fist-sized specimens. Bring a hand lens to identify chalcedony's waxy luster and subtle banding. Early morning light makes spotting specimens easier on the open terrain, and pack out all material you collect within the 25 lb daily limit.
terrain_notes: Elevation 5975 feet in open sagebrush desert with gentle slopes. Terrain is relatively flat with shallow washes and low rocky outcrops. Walking distance from parking typically ranges 0.5 to 2 miles depending on specific collecting spots. Footing is stable with scattered desert pavement and loose gravels. No shade or water available on-site.
best_season: April - June and September - November. The high elevation (nearly 6000 feet) means summers can be hot and dry, while winters occasionally see snow or cold temperatures. Spring and fall offer ideal 50-70 degree F conditions for extended collecting trips.
written_directions: From Mina, head south or east on Nevada State Route 773 or local county roads into the surrounding BLM land. The Mina area and immediate vicinity offer multiple pullouts and access points. Travel is generally possible via passenger vehicle on maintained county roads, though specific collecting spots may require short hiking from road pullouts. Contact the Battle Mountain District BLM office for current road conditions and specific access points.
road_conditions: Paved state highway access. Local county roads to collecting areas are generally passable by passenger vehicle, though conditions vary seasonally. Dirt pullouts available at multiple locations.
parking_notes: Informal parking at BLM pullouts along access roads. No developed parking area or facilities. Multiple spots available with capacity for 2-6 vehicles each.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Collect only from surface and weathered outcrops - do not dig or remove intact rock formations.
rules:
Personal use collection only
Maximum 25 pounds per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act
No commercial collection allowed
No motorized excavation or heavy equipment
Surface collection only - no digging into intact formations
Pack out all collected material
nearest_services: Mina (town) is at the site location with basic services. Tonopah, approximately 75 miles south, has full amenities including gas, food, lodging, and medical services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers with modest fitness. Gentle slopes and open desert make walking easy. Unpaved BLM roads to parking areas accessible by passenger vehicle. Short walks of 0.5 to 2 miles from vehicle to collecting areas. Sturdy footwear recommended due to scattered rocks and rough ground. No wheelchair access to remote collecting spots.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding near Mina Nevada?
faq.answer: The Mina area is known for quality chalcedony, quartz, agate, and jasper specimens. Chalcedony is the most common find here, often displaying translucent white to pale tan coloring with subtle banding. Agate and jasper pieces range from small nodules to substantial specimens suitable for cabochons or display.

faq.question: Is a permit required for rockhounding at Mina BLM land?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding on BLM land near Mina. Collection is free under the Federal Land Policy and Management Act, with a personal use limit of 25 pounds per day. Commercial collection and motorized excavation are not allowed.

faq.question: Where exactly should I search for gemstones around Mina?
faq.answer: Focus your search on washes, bajadas, and margins where seasonal water flow has concentrated lighter gemstones. Rocky outcrops and hillsides also yield good material. Early morning offers the best light for spotting translucent chalcedony and banded agate specimens. Access pullouts along Nevada State Route 773 or local county roads to reach productive collecting areas.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding near Mina?
faq.answer: April through June and September through November are ideal. Mina sits at nearly 6000 feet elevation, so summers can be hot and dry while winters occasionally bring snow. Spring and fall temperatures in the 50-70 degree F range make for comfortable collecting conditions during extended trips.

faq.question: Is rockhounding near Mina Nevada suitable for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Yes, absolutely. The easy terrain, free access, and abundant surface specimens make this site perfect for beginners and families with children ages 6 and up. The gentle slopes and open sagebrush desert allow safe exploration, and you can find quality gemstones without special equipment or advanced skills.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding at Mina?
faq.answer: Basic tools include a hand lens to identify chalcedony and agate details, a small pry bar or digging tool for surface collection, and a collection bag. Bring sturdy hiking boots for the rocky desert terrain. Essential items are sun protection, a hat, plenty of water, and a map or GPS unit. A 25 lb capacity backpack helps you stay within the daily collection limit.

meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding near Mina Nevada. Collect chalcedony, agate, and quartz. No permit required. 25 lb daily limit. Easy access for families.
